text:
SYNTHETIC FIXTURE — NOT A REAL PUC FILING
Virginia State Corporation Commission
Docket PUR-2024-00001 (example)
Re: Cost Allocation Methodology for Large Customer Loads

Page 1 — Introduction

The Company submits this petition for approval of a revised cost
allocation methodology applicable to large customer loads taking
service under tariff schedules GS-3 and GS-4. The petition addresses
the allocation of generation, transmission, and distribution costs
among customer classes, with specific attention to data-center loads
that have grown materially during the prior rate period.

The proposed methodology continues the embedded cost-of-service
framework approved in Case No. PUE-2017-00001, with three changes.
First, the allocation of generation demand-related costs uses the
twelve coincident peak (12-CP) method rather than the single
coincident peak previously in effect. Second, the allocation of
distribution demand-related costs uses the non-coincident peak
(NCP) method, reflecting the local nature of distribution sizing.
Third, a new customer subclass is created for high-load-factor
data-center customers with contract demands above 25 megawatts.

Page 2 — Allocation of Generation Costs

Generation demand-related costs are allocated to customer classes in
proportion to each class's contribution to the twelve coincident
monthly system peaks (12-CP). This change recognizes the diversity
of high-load-factor customers, including data centers, whose
contribution to coincident peaks is materially below their share of
total energy. Allocation results using 12-CP and 1-CP are presented
in Schedule G-1.

Energy-related generation costs continue to be allocated on the
basis of class energy usage, as in the prior methodology. The
allocation factors are computed from the calendar-year 2023 billing
determinants set forth in Workpaper WP-G-2. No change is proposed to
the allocation of fuel costs, which remain subject to the fuel
factor proceeding under Case No. PUR-2023-00099.

The Company recommends that data-center loads be assigned to the new
GS-4 subclass for purposes of generation cost allocation. Data-center
contract demands above 25 MW present sizing implications that differ
from general service customers and warrant a distinct allocation
treatment. The GS-4 subclass receives allocation factors computed on
the same 12-CP basis as other classes, with class-specific load
research data substituted for the system load research sample.

Page 3 — Allocation of Transmission and Distribution Costs

Transmission demand-related costs are allocated using the single
coincident peak (1-CP) method, consistent with the regional
transmission organization's billing approach. The Company's
transmission charges from PJM are passed through to customer classes
on the 1-CP basis described in PJM Open Access Transmission Tariff,
Schedule 8. No change is proposed to the transmission allocation
methodology in this proceeding.

Distribution demand-related costs are allocated to customer classes
using the non-coincident peak (NCP) method. NCP allocation reflects
that distribution facilities are sized to the local peak demand of
the served class rather than to the system-wide peak. The Company's
distribution cost allocation factors are derived from class-specific
non-coincident peak demands recorded during the calendar-year 2023
test period, as set forth in Workpaper WP-D-1.

Customer-related distribution costs (services, meters, and a portion
of billing) are allocated using customer counts weighted by the
relative cost of serving each class, as in the prior methodology.
Weights are derived from the minimum-system study set forth in
Schedule D-3. No change is proposed to the customer-cost allocation
methodology.

Page 4 — Data-Center Subclass and Rate Design Implications

The Company proposes a new GS-4 subclass for high-load-factor
data-center customers with contract demands above 25 megawatts. The
subclass receives separate cost allocation factors and is the
foundation for the rate design proposed in Schedule R-1. The
subclass is supported by the load research data and the cost
allocation methodology described in this petition.

The Company requests that the Commission approve the proposed cost
allocation methodology for use in this and subsequent base rate
proceedings, subject to revisions in load research data and billing
determinants. The Company further requests that the Commission
approve the GS-4 subclass and the related rate design described in
the testimony of witness Carter (Exhibit JBC-1).

No change is proposed to the allocation of administrative and
general (A&G) expenses, which continue to be allocated on the basis
of labor ratios as in the prior methodology. No change is proposed
to the allocation of depreciation expense, which follows the plant
account allocations described in Workpaper WP-A-1.
